conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions. Let me start with the first one:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. My son Alex is 6 years old, and I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have one child, Alex, who is 6 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few more questions.

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. That's helpful to know.

**Do you currently live with Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Thank you for confirming that.

**Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation, or has he been in any of these situations for an extended period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he was in care for a little while, but it wasn't for very long - just a couple of months or so.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. This is important information as it can affect Child Benefit eligibility.

**Can you tell me more specifically - is Alex currently in care now, or has he returned home? And approximately how long was he in care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's back home with me now. He was in care for about 7 weeks in total.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. It's good to know Alex is back home with you now.

Since Alex was in care for about 7 weeks (which is less than 8 weeks) and is now living back at home with you, let me ask you a few final questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Just one more question to complete the assessment:

**Are you responsible for Alex - meaning you're caring for him and paying for his needs such as food, clothes, and other expenses?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm the one looking after him and paying for everything he needs - his food, clothes, all of that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, let me make my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 6 years old, which is under the age limit of 16 (or 20 if in approved education/training)
- You live in the UK
- Alex lives at home with you
- You are responsible for him and paying for his needs
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- Although Alex was in care for about 7 weeks, this is under the 8-week threshold after which Child Benefit would normally stop, and he's now back home with you

You meet all the key eligibility requirements for Child Benefit. If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit through the government website or by contacting HMRC.

**Please note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement for Child Benefit. You live in the UK and Alex lives with you at home. You are responsible for Alex, paying for his food, clothes and other needs. Although Alex was in care for approximately 7 weeks, this is under the 8-week threshold, so Child Benefit would have continued during that period. Alex is now back home living with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. You meet all the eligibility criteria: you're responsible for a child under 16, you live with them in the UK, and no one else is claiming for the same child.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 6-year-old son Alex. They live in the UK, Alex lives at home with them, they are responsible for Alex's care and expenses, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. Alex's previous time in care (7 weeks) was under the 8-week limit and he has now returned home, so this does not affect current eligibility.

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. You meet all the eligibility criteria: you're responsible for a child under 16, you live with them in the UK, and no one else is claiming for the same child.

Alex's previous time in care (7 weeks) was under the 8-week limit and he has now returned home, so this does not affect current eligibility.
